posts.title: Scotty's Diner, Route 30, Pennsylvania, 1942
posts.text:
Scotty's Diner, Route 30, Wilkinsburg, Pennsylvania. Built in 1942 from a prefab Silk City model — one of the last stainless steel diners manufactured by the Paterson Vehicle Company in New Jersey.

These diners were built like railroad cars. Stainless steel panels, tile floors, Formica counters, a row of red vinyl stools. They shipped whole on flatbed trucks and dropped onto a concrete pad.

Silk City built about 1,700 diners between 1927 and 1964. Most are gone. Scotty's survived because someone kept it open.

John Margolies, 1989. Library of Congress, Margolies Archive.

tweets.text:

Silk City made about 1,700 diners between 1927 and 1964. Most are gone. This one survived because someone kept it open.

John Margolies, 1989. Library of Congress.

posts.title: Mickey's Diner, St. Paul, Minnesota, 1937
posts.text:
Mickey's Diner, 36 West 9th Street, St. Paul, Minnesota. Built 1937-39 by the Jerry O'Mahony Company. Open 24 hours since 1939. Never closed.

The building is a single railroad-car-style diner, 50 feet long by 10 feet wide. Yellow and red porcelain enamel exterior. Art Deco lettering. Eleven stools at a counter. No tables.

In 1983 it was the first diner in the US listed on the National Register of Historic Places. They've served the same menu for 80+ years.

John Margolies, photographer. Library of Congress, Margolies Archive.

posts.title: Astro Motel Sign, Fresno, California
posts.text:
Astro Motel sign, Motel Drive, Fresno, California. Sometime in the 1960s, a motel owner on a strip called Motel Drive decided the future was space. Painted an atom-age starburst, added a rocket-shaped pole, and named it the Astro.

Motel Drive in Fresno had 30+ motels in a two-mile stretch. They competed on signage. The signs got taller. The colors got louder. By the 1970s, the interstate bypassed them all.

Most of the signs are gone now. The Astro's is still there.

John Margolies, photographer. Library of Congress, Margolies Archive.

posts.title: Tascosa Drive-In Theater, Amarillo, Texas
posts.text:
Tascosa Drive-In Theater, NE 24th and Dumas Drive, Amarillo, Texas. The screen is five stories tall. The marquee is hand-painted.

At the peak in 1958, America had 4,063 drive-in theaters. They were cheap to build — a screen, a field, and a speaker box on every post. Families brought blankets. Teenagers brought each other.

By 2023 there were about 300 left. Most were killed by daylight saving time (later sunsets meant later start times meant fewer customers) and rising land values (a 10-acre lot in a growing city is worth more as a strip mall).

John Margolies, photographer. Library of Congress, Margolies Archive.

posts.title: Candy Motel Sign, Route 99, Fresno, California
posts.text:
Candy Motel sign, Route 99, Fresno, California. Pink and white. Vacancy. The name on the sign is in a cursive script that nobody designs anymore.

Route 99 was California's main north-south highway before I-5. Every town along it had a motel row. The signs were designed to be read at 45 mph from 500 feet away. Big, bright, simple. One word if possible.

The Candy Motel was one of hundreds. The sign was one of thousands. John Margolies spent 40 years photographing them before they disappeared.

John Margolies, photographer. Library of Congress, Margolies Archive.

posts.title: Victory Drive-In Theater, Van Nuys, California
posts.text:
Victory Drive-In Theater, Victory Boulevard west of Coldwater Canyon, Van Nuys, California. The screen tower has a painted mural — mountains, a sunset, the name in script.

Van Nuys in the San Fernando Valley was the capital of drive-in culture. Within a 20-mile radius in the 1950s there were more than 60 drive-ins. On a Friday night every one of them was full.

The Victory closed in 1996. The lot is a shopping center now. The mural is gone.

John Margolies, photographer. Library of Congress, Margolies Archive.

posts.title: Colored Motel Sign, Route 80, Statesboro, Georgia
posts.text:
Colored Motel sign, Route 80, Statesboro, Georgia. The sign says what it means. This was a motel for Black travelers in the segregated South.

Before the Civil Rights Act of 1964, Black motorists couldn't stop at most motels, restaurants, or gas stations. Victor Hugo Green published The Negro Motorist Green Book starting in 1936 — a directory of safe places to eat, sleep, and get gas.

The Green Book listed about 9,600 establishments at its peak. This motel on Route 80 was probably one of them.

John Margolies, photographer. Library of Congress, Margolies Archive.
